header.title: Array
header.description: An array stores elements in contiguous memory, giving O(1) index access and update. It is the single most used data structure in competitive programming and system design — mastering it unlocks sliding window, two-pointer, and prefix-sum patterns.

howItWorks.title: How arrays work
items: Memory is laid out contiguously: element i lives at base_address + i × element_size, so any index is a single arithmetic jump — O(1).
Reading or writing by index is always constant time regardless of array length.
Appending to the end (push) is O(1) amortized: the buffer doubles when full, so the average cost across n appends is still O(1).
Inserting or deleting in the middle requires shifting every element after that point, making it O(n) in the worst case.
Searching for a value without an index requires a full linear scan — O(n).

files.code:
# ─── Arrays (lists) in Python ───────────────────────────────────────────────
#
# Python's list is a dynamic array backed by a C array of object pointers.
# It over-allocates on growth to keep amortized O(1) for append.
# Use array.array or numpy.ndarray when you need a true typed contiguous array.

arr = [10, 20, 30, 40]

# ── O(1) — Read by index ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
x = arr[2]          # 30
y = arr[-1]         # 40  (negative indexing wraps from the end)

# ── O(1) — Update by index ───────────────────────────────────────────────
arr[1] = 25         # [10, 25, 30, 40]

# ── O(1) amortized — Append to end ───────────────────────────────────────
arr.append(50)      # [10, 25, 30, 40, 50]

# ── O(1) amortized — Remove from end ─────────────────────────────────────
last = arr.pop()    # last = 50, arr = [10, 25, 30, 40]

# ── O(n) — Insert at arbitrary index ─────────────────────────────────────
# Every element from index onward is shifted one slot to the right in C,
# then the new object pointer is written. Fast in practice (C loop),
# but still linear in the number of elements shifted.
arr.insert(1, 15)   # [10, 15, 25, 30, 40]

# ── O(n) — Delete at arbitrary index ─────────────────────────────────────
arr.pop(2)          # removes index 2 (value 25) → [10, 15, 30, 40]
# del arr[2] is equivalent and slightly faster (no return value overhead)

# ── O(n) — Linear search ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
idx = arr.index(30) # 2  (raises ValueError if not found)
found = 30 in arr   # True  (uses __contains__, same O(n) scan)

# ── Slicing — O(k) where k = slice length ────────────────────────────────
# Creates a shallow copy of the sub-range. Does NOT modify the original.
sub = arr[1:3]      # [15, 30]
rev = arr[::-1]     # [40, 30, 15, 10]  (reversed copy)

# ── List comprehensions — concise O(n) transforms ────────────────────────
doubled = [v * 2 for v in arr]          # [20, 30, 60, 80]
evens   = [v for v in arr if v % 2 == 0] # [10, 30, 40]

# ── Practical patterns ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Two-pointer — O(n), O(1) space
def two_sum_sorted(nums, target):
    left, right = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while left < right:
        s = nums[left] + nums[right]
        if s == target:
            return left, right
        elif s < target:
            left += 1
        else:
            right -= 1
    return None

# Sliding window — O(n), O(1) space
def max_subarray_sum(nums, k):
    window = sum(nums[:k])
    best = window
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window += nums[i] - nums[i - k]
        best = max(best, window)
    return best

# Prefix sums — O(n) build, O(1) range queries
def build_prefix(nums):
    prefix = [0] * (len(nums) + 1)
    for i, v in enumerate(nums):
        prefix[i + 1] = prefix[i] + v
    return prefix
# range_sum(l, r) = prefix[r + 1] - prefix[l]

footerAlerts.title: When arrays shine
items: Random access by index — O(1) makes arrays perfect for lookup tables and matrices.
Sliding window / two-pointer — fixed-size window slides in O(n) with no extra allocations.
Prefix / suffix preprocessing — build in O(n), answer range queries in O(1).
Cache-friendly iteration — contiguous memory means each cache line loads several elements at once.

footerAlerts.title: When to consider alternatives
items: Frequent insert/delete in the middle → use a linked list or deque.
Frequent search without index → use a hash set/map for O(1) lookup.
Unknown size with many inserts at the front → use a deque.
